metadata.summary: Defines and configures five Scrum events for team size, timezone spread, and PM tool. Ceremony overhead stays below 10% of sprint capacity with action-item closure tracking.

content.title: Agile Ceremonies Setup
content.summary: Defines, configures, and continuously improves the five Scrum events — Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Retrospective, and Backlog Refinement — for a team's specific size, timezone spread, and PM tool. Total ceremony overhead must stay at or below 10% of sprint capacity. Each event has a mandatory time-box, a single sprint goal, and tracked action-item closure.

why.p: Ceremonies decay without explicit structure: standups become status reports, retrospectives surface the same complaints sprint after sprint, reviews turn into internal demos without real stakeholder feedback. Time-boxing plus outcome tracking turns ceremonies from ritual into an actual feedback loop. Untracked retro actions means the team never improves.

list.item: Bootstrapping Scrum for a new team: defining cadence, agendas, time-boxes, PM tool wiring.
Migrating a waterfall or ad-hoc team to a structured ceremony rhythm.
Tuning ceremonies that have decayed (silent retros, status-report standups, drifting reviews).
Setting up async / hybrid ceremonies for distributed teams with wide timezone spread.
Configuring Jira, Linear, or Azure DevOps to mirror the ceremony cadence.
Solo or 2-person teams — overhead exceeds value; lightweight async check-ins suffice.
Continuous-delivery Kanban teams with WIP limits and SLA dashboards — ceremonies become anti-patterns.
Teams with zero psychological safety — retros become blame sessions and worsen things before they help; fix safety first.

section.name: Time-Box Reference and Core Rules

rule.statement: Never run a ceremony past its time-box; end on time even if the agenda is incomplete — incomplete items become backlog.
rule.rationale: Ceremonies that overrun erode trust in the process; the team learns that the schedule is fictional and stops preparing.

section.p: Time-boxes by sprint length: Planning: 2h/4h/8h for 1wk/2wk/4wk. Daily: 15min always. Review: 1h/2h/4h. Retro: 45min/90min/3h. Refinement: 1-2h/2-4h/4-8h.

rule.statement: Write one sprint goal per sprint, before selecting backlog items. Reject a goal that does not answer "what changes for users?"
rule.rationale: Without a sprint goal, the daily standup becomes a status report and the team has no shared criterion for success. LLM-generated goals tend to be vacuous ("complete sprint backlog") — reject them by the same test.

rule.statement: Apply Definition of Ready at refinement (acceptance criteria, dependencies, estimate, fits in sprint, testable). Apply Definition of Done at planning. Both must be agreed before the first sprint begins.
rule.rationale: Without DoR, sprint scope is undecided at planning start. Without DoD, done means different things to developer, tester, and stakeholder.

rule.statement: Track retro actions as backlog items tagged with their originating sprint. If closure rate falls below 70% over three consecutive sprints, declare the retro format broken and change it.
rule.rationale: Retros where actions are never implemented produce cynicism faster than no retro at all. The 70% threshold is a measurable signal that the format is working.

rule.statement: Total ceremony time per person must not exceed 10% of sprint capacity. Audit the calendar every quarter; kill any recurring meeting where more than 20% of attendees would not voluntarily opt in.
rule.rationale: Beyond 10%, the team trades delivery time for coordination overhead without proportional benefit.

section.name: Sprint Planning

rule.statement: Split planning into two parts: Part 1 (what) — review goal, top items, acceptance criteria. Part 2 (how) — break stories into tasks, estimate, identify dependencies, commit. Each part gets equal time.
rule.rationale: Mixing what and how in a single session causes scope confusion; the team commits before understanding the work.

section.p: Jira setup: backlog view + sprint-planning mode, filter "status = Ready AND sprint IS EMPTY", show story points + AC + priority. Linear setup: new cycle with dates, backlog filter "state:backlog", priority sort.

rule.statement: Prefer walk-the-board format over three-questions format: start from the rightmost column, discuss each in-flight item, identify blocks. Focus on flow, not individuals.
rule.rationale: Walk-the-board surfaces WIP age and blocking dependencies visually; the classic three-questions format produces individual status reports the team reads serially.

rule.statement: Update the board in real time during standup; never let a standup pass without moving cards. The tool is the record of the event.
rule.rationale: A standup that doesn't touch the board is theater; the board and the conversation must stay synchronized.

section.name: Sprint Review

rule.statement: Sprint Review must include real users or external stakeholders, not only the dev team. An internal-only review is waste.
rule.rationale: The purpose of the review is stakeholder feedback that adjusts the backlog; internal audiences cannot provide it. Stakeholder participation is mandatory per Scrum Guide 2020.

section.p: Agenda: welcome 5min, sprint goal recap 5min, demos 45min, feedback 15min, backlog discussion 15min, next preview 5min. Metrics to display: sprint goal met, story points completed, velocity trend.

rule.statement: Rotate retro format every 4-6 sprints (Start-Stop-Continue, 4Ls, Sailboat, Mad-Sad-Glad). Rotate the facilitator each sprint.
rule.rationale: Fixed format produces diminishing returns; rotation keeps the exercise fresh and distributes facilitation skill across the team.

section.name: Backlog Refinement

rule.statement: Use Planning Poker for estimation: everyone votes secretly, reveal simultaneously, discuss only the outliers, re-vote if needed. Consensus must be recorded in the tool before the session ends.
rule.rationale: Simultaneous reveal prevents anchoring on a first estimate; discussing outliers surfaces missing context without silencing quieter team members.

section.p: T-shirt sizing map: XS=1pt, S=2-3pt, M=5pt, L=8pt, XL=13+ (must be split before committing). Any item over L at refinement is rejected from the next sprint until split.

rule.statement: Async standup prompts must focus on goal-progress and blockers, not "what did you do yesterday". Reject prompts that produce individual status reports.
rule.rationale: Status questions produce status answers; goal-progress questions keep the team aligned on the sprint goal rather than individual task lists.

rule.statement: Use tz-aware scheduling for async standups; recalculate after daylight-saving transitions. Naive UTC offsets break twice a year.
rule.rationale: A standup that works in March may exclude APAC or Eastern Europe after the DST shift. Encode acceptable hours per participant in the bot config.

rule.statement: Maximum one sync ceremony per day for distributed teams; Sprint Planning and Retrospective may share a day, but no more than two sync events back-to-back.
rule.rationale: Zoom fatigue compounds across sessions; more than one long sync per day collapses participation quality by the second event.

rule.statement: Any global recurring meeting must either rotate its time across quarters or run two sessions. Document the rotation schedule in the team calendar.
rule.rationale: A fixed "Tue 10:00 UTC" permanently excludes APAC time zones from meaningful participation.

rule.statement: Run a ceremony health survey (1-5 scale) every quarter. Track: time-box compliance, sprint-goal-met rate, retro action closure rate, velocity stability. Trigger a retro on ceremonies if any metric scores below 3.
rule.rationale: Without measurement, ceremony decay is invisible until the team openly refuses to attend. Quantified health scores make the problem surfaceable before that point.

antipattern.description: Ceremony rituals re-imported when a team has moved to flow-based delivery.
antipattern.reason: Sprint cadence imposed on a Kanban team adds overhead without benefit; the team "does Scrum ceremonies for Kanban" and cargo-cults both methods badly.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Three useful agent surfaces: (1) one-time setup — generate ceremony cadence, agendas, tool config (Jira board, Linear cycle, ADO sprint) from team profile (size, timezone spread, sprint length). (2) Ongoing facilitation — async standup bot prompts (Geekbot / Standuply / Slack workflow), retro board templates per sprint, agenda generation tied to sprint metrics. (3) Continuous improvement — post-sprint analysis: did the team meet sprint goal? velocity trend? action-item closure rate? Then propose ceremony tweaks. Keep agents away from authoring sprint goals or assigning work — those are PO / team decisions.

Time-box discipline collapses without an active facilitator; ceremonies bloat 2-3x and the team blames "agile" rather than facilitation. Async standups become status reports nobody reads; lose the point of the event. Sprint reviews drift into "demo theatre" instead of real stakeholder feedback. Retros that don't track action-item completion produce same complaints every sprint, then team gives up. Tool-driven ceremonies (Jira-only) miss the conversational value; tool is record, not the event.

section.name: Prompt Pattern for Agent Setup
section.p: Inputs: team_size=7, sprint_length=2w, timezone_spread="UTC-5 to UTC+3", delivery_model="scrum", tool="jira", remote_pct=80. Output Markdown bundle: ceremony_cadence (table: event, time-box, day, mode sync/async), per_event_agenda (sprint-planning, daily, review, retro, refinement), jira_config (sprint dates, board columns, quick filters, dashboard widgets), standup_bot_config (Geekbot YAML), definition_of_ready, definition_of_done starter. Hard constraints: total ceremony overhead per person ≤ 8% of sprint capacity. Do NOT propose more than 2 sync ceremonies for high timezone spread.

list.item: Cap total ceremony time at ≤ 10% of sprint capacity. If the team complains about meetings, audit the time-boxes first.
One *single* sprint goal per sprint, written before backlog selection. No goal = standup is status reporting.
Definition of Ready (DoR) at refinement, Definition of Done (DoD) at planning. Without both, sprint scope is mush.
Track completed retro actions as backlog items with the sprint they came from. < 70% closure rate = retros aren't working.
Rotate facilitators (standup, retro) — distributes facilitation skill, prevents Scrum-Master single point of failure.
For distributed teams: maximum one sync ceremony per day (planning + retro can be the same day); use async for standups.
Sprint Review must include real users / stakeholders, not just the dev team. Internal demo = waste.
Retro format rotation (Start-Stop-Continue → 4Ls → Sailboat → Mad-Sad-Glad) every 4-6 sprints to keep it fresh.
Ceremony health survey (1-5 score) every quarter — measure the ceremony, not just the work.
LLM defaults to Scrum vocabulary even when team is on Kanban / flow; it will invent sprints that don't exist. Anchor on `delivery_model` input.
Generated agendas often exceed time-box (60+45+45 = 150 min for a 2-week sprint review is overkill); enforce sum ≤ time-box.
Async standup prompts written by LLMs default to status questions ("what did you do yesterday"); push toward goal-progress + blocker focus.
Retro action items often lack owner / due date / measurable outcome — agent emits "improve communication" not "implement standup template by sprint N+1, owner @x". Force structured output.
Tool-config generation: Jira / Linear / ADO have rate-limited APIs and irreversible side effects (deleted boards). Always dry-run first; never let agent delete boards.
Timezone math: agent forgets daylight-saving transitions; standup that worked in March breaks in November. Use tz-aware library, not naive UTC offsets.
LLMs invent "Scrum guide" rules ("daily must be standing") that aren't in the actual Scrum Guide (2020). Cite version.
Sprint goal generation by LLM tends to be vacuous ("complete sprint backlog"); reject if it doesn't pass the "what changes for users?" test.

template.purpose: Sprint planning artifact: goal, capacity table, backlog, risks.
Retro artifact: metrics, went-well, improve, action items with owners.

script.purpose: Create and start a Jira sprint with goal, dates, and top ready issues via jira-cli.
